nativeRange.habitat: Steep seaward reefs over sand patches, rubble, and hard substrate, generally at depths of 25–90 m

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding primarily on zooplankton and small crustaceans suspended in the water column. Aquarium specimens have been known to accept frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and high-quality marine pellets. Feeding 2–3 times daily in small portions is generally advised.

summary: The Helfrichi Firefish is a deepwater dartfish prized for its vivid yellow-orange face that fades to lavender and white along the body. It is generally considered reef-safe and peaceful, though it tends to be shy and will jump from open aquariums, making a tight-fitting lid essential.
careNotes:
The Helfrichi Firefish displays a vivid yellow-orange head that transitions to a lavender-purple body, making it one of the more visually striking dartfish available to reef keepers. It tends to inhabit the lower portion of the tank, frequently retreating to crevices or burrows among live rock and rubble when startled or stressed.

A secure, tight-fitting lid or screen cover is strongly advised, as this species has been known to jump from open aquaria.

Two individuals will generally only coexist peacefully if they form a bonded mated pair. Single specimens or unpaired individuals have been known to fight aggressively with others of the same species, so keeping more than one is not advised unless a proven pair is available.

Varied diet enrichment is generally recommended, as color loss over time has been reported in aquarium specimens fed a restricted or unenriched diet. Rotating frozen foods such as mysis shrimp, enriched brine shrimp, and cyclops tends to help maintain coloration.

This species naturally inhabits deeper reef waters in the wild (25–90 m), which may account for its preference for shaded hiding spots and its reported sensitivity to bright, direct lighting in some aquariums.
breedingNotes: Has been reported to form monogamous pairs in the wild. Captive breeding has been documented only rarely; larvae require specialized plankton-based feeding regimes and are not routinely raised in home aquaria. Captive pairs have been observed spawning but rearing success rates remain very low.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive spawning has been observed in bonded pairs but larval rearing is extremely difficult and not routinely achieved in home aquaria.
